items.question: Who uses loss surface intuition in machine learning?
items.answer: ML engineers, researchers, and students use it to reason about training dynamics, optimizer choice, learning-rate schedules, initialization, and why different runs reach different solutions—even when the loss function is fixed.

items.question: What is a loss surface?
items.answer: The graph of a loss function L(θ) over model parameters θ: each parameter vector is a location and L(θ) is the height (or contour level) at that location.

items.question: When is a 2D contour plot a faithful guide?
items.answer: When you deliberately slice or project a tiny model (one or two free parameters) or plot random directions near the current weights; it is least faithful when treated as the full million-dimensional landscape of a production neural net.

items.question: Where does optimization happen on the loss surface?
items.answer: At the current parameter point θₜ; the optimizer reads local slope (gradient), chooses a step direction and size, and moves to θₜ₊₁ with (hopefully) lower loss—tracing a path across the surface.

items.question: Why visualize landscapes instead of only plotting loss vs epoch?
items.answer: Loss-vs-epoch shows altitude along one path, not the surrounding terrain. Surface views reveal multiple valleys, saddle structure, and curvature that explain why learning rate, initialization, and optimizer change outcomes.

items.question: How do you sketch a loss surface for a two-parameter model?
items.answer: Choose a grid of (θ₁, θ₂) values, evaluate L(θ) on training data at each grid point, then plot a 3D surface or 2D contour map with θ₁ and θ₂ as axes and L as height or color.

items.question: Convex loss surface vs non-convex loss surface: what changes for optimization?
items.answer: Convex: every local minimum is global; gradient descent with appropriate step size converges to the same bowl. Non-convex: many critical points (local minima, saddles); different initializations and optimizers can land in different valleys with different loss and generalization.

items.question: Loss surface plot vs training loss curve: what does each show?
items.answer: Surface plot (slice/contour): geometry of L(θ) over parameter space at a snapshot or grid—multiple directions and heights. Training curve: loss only along the time-ordered optimizer path; compact for monitoring but hides surrounding terrain.

items.question: What if you start gradient descent from two different random initializations on a non-convex surface?
items.answer: The two runs typically follow different downhill paths and can settle in different local minima or basins, yielding similar or different training loss and often different test performance—even with identical data, loss, and architecture.

items.question: What if the learning rate is too large relative to valley curvature?
items.answer: Update steps overshoot the valley floor: loss oscillates or diverges upward on the surface, appearing as unstable training even though a good minimum exists nearby.
